title: Preventing Violence Against Women by Illegal Aliens Act
short_title: Preventing Violence Against Women by Illegal Aliens Act
plain_summary: This bill makes new rules about which non-citizens can come to or stay in the United States. If a non-citizen has been convicted of or admits to crimes like stalking, child abuse, sex crimes, domestic violence, or breaking protection orders, they cannot enter the country. Non-citizens already living in the United States who commit these same crimes must be removed from the country. The bill adds more types of crimes that can lead to deportation, especially sex crimes and expanded definitions of domestic violence.
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on the Judiciary.

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Preventing Violence Against Women by Illegal Aliens Act.

sections.title: Section 2 Inadmissibility and Deportability Related to Sex Offenses, Domestic Violence, Stalking, Child Abuse, or Violation of Protection Order
sections.text: This section changes the Immigration and Nationality Act to add new rules about which non-citizens cannot enter the United States and which ones must be removed from the country. Part A adds rules about who cannot be allowed into the United States. It says that any non-citizen who has been found guilty of a sex crime, who admits they committed a sex crime, or who admits they did things that make up the basic parts of a sex crime cannot enter the United States. A sex crime is defined the same way as in the Adam Walsh Child Protection and Safety Act. This also applies to anyone who planned to commit such crimes with others. The law also says that non-citizens cannot enter if they have been found guilty of, admit to, or admit doing acts that make up domestic violence, stalking, child abuse or neglect or abandonment, or violating a protection order. Domestic violence means harming someone you live with or have a close relationship with. Stalking means following or bothering someone in a way that scares them. A protection order is a court order that tells someone to stay away from another person to keep them safe. Part B changes rules about removing people from the United States. It updates the definition of domestic violence to include any crime that counts as domestic violence under federal law, even if the state or local area does not get federal money for programs about domestic violence. It also adds a new rule that says any non-citizen who has been found guilty of a sex crime or planning to commit a sex crime with others must be removed from the United States.
